content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 8, 1991

Claude L. Biddie, City Attorney
City of Grass Valley
125 East Main Street
Grass Valley, CA 95945

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-142

Dear Mr. Biddie:

You have requested advice on the disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Your letter initially
requested a written opinion pursuant to Section 83114. Your
office contacted us during the week of April 8 and clarified that
you are merely seeking written advice.

QUESTION

Does the Peace Officer Bill of Rights exempt the city's chief
of police from the disclosure provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

There is nothing in the Act which grants relief to public
officials who believe disclosure of their financial interests is
protected by other laws.

FACTS

The conflict of interest code for the city of Grass Valley
designates certain positions within the city and assigns
corresponding disclosure categories. The chief of police is a
department head charged with the responsibility of managing the
personnel, budget, equipment and materials of the police

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Page Two
May 8, 1991

department and is specifically involved in purchasing equipment,
maintenance agreements, setting the budget, salaries, etc. Since
the position of chief of police is the type of position which
makes or participates in the making of decisions, the city council
included that position in the code.

The police chief for the city of Grass Valley believes his
position should be exempt from disclosure because such disclosure
constitutes an unwarranted invasion of privacy. He also contends
that the Peace Officer Bill of Rights (Government Code Section
3308) protects peace officers from disclosing economic
interests.

ANALYSIS

The constitutionality of the Act's disclosure provisions has
already been considered and upheld. The right of privacy does
not prevail over the right of the public to an honest and
impartial government, as implemented by legislation requiring a
variety of public officials and candidates to disclose their
financial interests. See County of Nevada v MacMillan (1974) 11
Cal.3d 662 and Hays v Wood (1979) 25 Cal.3d 772; 160 Cal. Rptr.
102, 603 P.2d 19.

In regards to the police chief's contention that Government
Code Section 3308 protects his right to privacy, Section 3308
provides that:

"No public safety officer shall be required or
requested for purposes of job assignments or other
personnel action to disclose any item of his property,
income, assets, source of income, debts or personal or
domestic expenditures (including those of any member of
his family or household) unless such information is
obtained or required under state law or proper legal
procedure, tends to indicate a conflict of interest with
respect to the performance of his official duties, or is
necessary for the employing agency to ascertain the
desirability of assigning the public safety officer to a
specialized unit in which there is a strong possibility
that bribes or other improper inducements may be
offered." (Emphasis added).

Section 3308 specifically indicates that such disclosure is
proper if required by state law. The Political Reform Act is
state law.

There is nothing in the Act which grants relief to public
officials who believe disclosure of their financial interests is
protected by other laws. The Act's disclosure provisions apply to
judges who hear and sentence criminal cases as well as to the
Governor and members of the Legislature. The chief of police is
subject to the disclosure provisions of the Act.

Page Three
May 8, 1991


If there is anything further you need, please feel free to ——
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel


By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SH/JET/jt

sections.question: Does the Peace Officer Bill of Rights exempt the city's chief of police from the disclosure provisions of the Act?
sections.conclusion: There is nothing in the Act which grants relief to public officials who believe disclosure of their financial interests is protected by other laws.

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

department and is specifically involved in purchasing equipment, maintenance agreements, setting the budget, salaries, etc. Since—the position of chief of police is the type of position which makes or participates in the making of decisions, the city council included that position in the code.

The police chief for the city of Grass Valley believes his position should be exempt from disclosure because such disclosure constitutes an unwarranted invasion of privacy. He also contends that the Peace Officer Bill of Rights (Government Code Section 3308) protects the peace officers from disclosing economic interests.
sections.analysis:
The constitutionality of the Act’s disclosure provisions has already been considered and upheld. The right of privacy does not prevail over the right of the public to an honest and impartial government, as implemented by legislation requiring a variety of public officials and candidates to disclose their financial interests. See County of Nevada v MacMillan (1974) 11 Cal. 3d 662 and Hays v Wood (1979) 25 Cal.3d 772; 160 Cal. Rptr. 102, 603 P.2d 19.

In regards to the police chief’s contention that Government Code Section 3308 protects his right to privacy, Section 3308 provides that:

"No public safety officer shall be required or requested for purposes of job assignments or other personnel action to disclose any item of his property, income, assets, source of income, debts or personal or domestic expenditures (including those of any member of his family or household) unless such information is obtained or required under state law or proper legal procedure, tends to indicate a conflict of interest with respect to the performance of his official duties, or is necessary for the employing agency to ascertain the desirability of assigning the public safety officer to a specialized unit in which there is a strong possibility that bribes or other improper inducements may be offered." (Emphasis added).

Section 3308 specifically indicates that such disclosure is proper if required by state law. The Political Reform Act is state law.

There is nothing in the Act which grants relief to public officials who believe disclosure of their financial interests is protected by other laws. The Act’s disclosure provisions apply to judges who hear and sentence criminal cases as well as to the Governor and members of the Legislature. The chief of police is subject to the disclosure provisions of the Act.

If there is anything further you need, please feel free to — contact me at (916) 322-5901.
